modules.title: Active Aerodynamics
modules.subtitle: The biggest change in F1 since ground effect

sections.content: In 2026, F1 introduced fully active front and rear wings. Unlike the fixed-position wings of previous eras, drivers can now adjust wing angles dynamically — maximizing downforce in corners and reducing drag on straights.
On straights, the rear wing flap opens to a near-flat position, cutting drag significantly and boosting top speed by up to 15 km/h. In corners, the flap closes to generate maximum downforce for grip.

sections.text: The active rear wing can reduce drag by up to 20% on straights — making it more effective than the old DRS system it replaces.

sections.content: Unlike DRS, active aero is NOT restricted to zones or gap requirements. Every driver can use it at any point on the track, making it a more level playing field.

sections.question: What does the active aero system primarily do on straights?
sections.explanation: On straights, the wing flattens to reduce drag and increase top speed.

sections.question: How does 2026 active aero differ from DRS?
options: It only works in designated zones
It requires being within 1 second of the car ahead
It's available to all drivers at any point
It's only for the rear wing
sections.explanation: Unlike DRS, active aero has no zone or gap restrictions — every driver can use it anywhere.

sections.content: The 2026 power unit features a much larger battery and motor (MGU-K), allowing drivers to deploy a 'Boost Mode' — a surge of additional electrical power for overtaking.

When a driver activates boost mode, the MGU-K delivers its maximum 350kW output, providing a noticeable acceleration advantage. However, energy is finite — drivers must manage their deployment carefully.

sections.text: The 2026 MGU-K produces 350kW (470hp) of electrical power — nearly matching the 400kW (540hp) from the combustion engine. It's the closest F1 has ever come to a true 50/50 hybrid.

sections.content: Unlike the push-to-pass systems in other racing series, F1's boost mode is integrated into the overall energy management strategy. Using boost defensively means less energy for the next attack.

sections.question: How much electrical power does the 2026 MGU-K produce?
sections.explanation: The 2026 MGU-K produces 350kW (470hp), nearly matching the combustion engine.

sections.content: The 2026 power unit is a fundamental redesign. The total output remains around 1000hp, but the split changes dramatically: roughly 540hp from the internal combustion engine (ICE) and 470hp from the electric motor (MGU-K).

The biggest change: the MGU-H (Motor Generator Unit - Heat) is GONE. This complex component harvested energy from exhaust gases and was a major barrier to new manufacturer entry. Without it, developing a competitive F1 power unit becomes more accessible.

sections.text: The MGU-H removal is partly why Ford (with Red Bull), Audi, and GM/Cadillac entered F1 for 2026. The simpler architecture reduced development costs by an estimated 30%.

sections.content: The engine remains a 1.6-litre V6 turbo hybrid, but with a simplified turbocharger (no longer coupled to the MGU-H). The battery is significantly larger to compensate for the lost MGU-H energy recovery.

sections.question: What component was removed from the 2026 power unit?
sections.explanation: The MGU-H (Motor Generator Unit - Heat) was removed to simplify the power unit and attract new manufacturers.

sections.content: The 2026 battery is roughly three times the energy capacity of the 2025 unit. This is necessary because without the MGU-H continuously harvesting energy, the car relies more heavily on stored electrical energy.

Drivers now have much more freedom in how they deploy electrical energy. The increased battery size allows for extended periods of full electrical boost, making overtaking windows longer and more strategic.

sections.text: The 2026 ERS can deploy up to 8.5 MJ of energy per lap — more than double the 4 MJ limit of the 2025 regulations.

sections.question: Why is the 2026 battery larger?
options: To compensate for the removed MGU-H
sections.explanation: Without the MGU-H harvesting exhaust energy, the larger battery compensates with more stored capacity.

sections.content: Pirelli continues as sole tyre supplier with the 18-inch wheel format introduced in 2022. The compound range (C1-C5) remains, with Pirelli selecting three compounds per Grand Prix.
Dry compounds: SOFT (red, fastest/least durable), MEDIUM (yellow, balanced), HARD (white, slowest/most durable). Plus INTERMEDIATE (green) and WET (blue) for rain.
Teams MUST use at least two different dry compounds during a race. The 'tyre cliff' — the sudden loss of performance — remains the key strategic variable.

sections.text: A soft tyre typically lasts 15-20 laps, a medium 25-35 laps, and a hard 35-50 laps — but this varies massively by circuit.

sections.question: Which is the softest dry compound?
sections.explanation: The Soft (Red) compound offers the most grip but wears out fastest.

sections.content: DRS (Drag Reduction System) — the overtaking aid that opened a rear wing flap in designated zones — has been REMOVED for 2026. It's been replaced by the full active aerodynamics system.

DRS required drivers to be within 1 second of the car ahead at a detection point, and could only be activated in designated zones. The new active aero has NO restrictions — any driver, any position, any part of the track.

sections.text: DRS was introduced in 2011 and lasted 15 seasons. It was always intended to be a temporary solution until cars could follow each other more closely.

sections.question: What replaced DRS in 2026?
sections.explanation: Active aero replaces DRS with a more comprehensive system available to all drivers.

modules.title: Sprint Weekend Format
modules.subtitle: The compressed race weekend with an extra short race

sections.content: Sprint weekends replace one practice session with a shorter Sprint Race (about 100km, roughly 1/3 of the full race distance). The weekend format is: FP1 → Sprint Qualifying → Sprint Race → Qualifying → Grand Prix.
Sprint Qualifying determines the Sprint Race grid. Standard Qualifying determines the Grand Prix grid. Sprint races award points to the top 8 finishers (8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1).

sections.question: How long is a Sprint Race?
sections.explanation: Sprint races are approximately 100km, about one-third of the full race distance.

modules.title: Cost Cap & Budget Regulations
modules.subtitle: How F1 limits spending to level the playing field

sections.content: Since 2021, F1 has had a cost cap limiting team spending. For 2026, the cap is approximately $135 million per season (excluding driver salaries, top-3 personnel salaries, and marketing costs).

Teams that exceed the cap face penalties ranging from fines to constructors' championship deductions. In 2022, Red Bull breached the cap by ~$1.8M and received a $7M fine plus a 10% wind tunnel reduction.

sections.text: Before the cost cap, top teams spent over $400 million per season. The cap has already helped midfield teams become more competitive.

sections.question: What is NOT excluded from the F1 cost cap?
sections.explanation: Car development is included in the cap. Driver salaries, marketing, and top executive pay are excluded.

modules.title: F1 Through the Ages
modules.subtitle: V10 screams, turbo beasts, and ground effect magic

sections.content: The first F1 championship was held in 1950. Since then, the sport has evolved through multiple engine eras: naturally-aspirated (1950-1988), first turbo era (1977-1988), V10 era (1995-2005), V8 era (2006-2013), and the current turbo-hybrid era (2014-present).
The V10 era (1995-2005) is often considered F1's golden age of engine sound — cars revved to 20,000+ RPM with an unforgettable scream. The V8 era brought cost controls and KERS (early hybrid).

Ground effect, where shaped floors create massive downforce, was first used in the late 1970s (Lotus 79), banned for safety reasons, then brought back in 2022 with the new regulations — leading to the closest racing in decades.

sections.text: Michael Schumacher holds the record for most wins in a season: 13 out of 18 races in 2004. Lewis Hamilton holds the all-time win record with 103+ victories.

modules.title: Flags & Race Director Signals
modules.subtitle: What each coloured flag means for drivers

sections.content: 🟢 Green Flag: Track is clear, racing can proceed. 🟡 Yellow: Danger ahead — slow down, no overtaking. Double yellow means be prepared to stop.
🔴 Red Flag: Session stopped. All cars must return to pit lane. 🔵 Blue Flag: A faster car is approaching to lap you — let them pass within 3 flag points or face a penalty.
🏁 Chequered Flag: Session over. ⬛🟧 Black and Orange: Your car has a mechanical problem — return to the pits. ⬛ Black Flag: You are disqualified.

sections.question: What does a blue flag mean?
options: Faster car approaching, let them pass
sections.explanation: Blue flags warn that a faster car is approaching to lap you. You must let them pass.

modules.title: Points System
modules.subtitle: How drivers and teams score championship points

sections.content: The top 10 finishers score points: 25-18-15-12-10-8-6-4-2-1. An additional bonus point is awarded for the fastest lap, but only if the driver finishes in the top 10.
Sprint races award points to the top 8: 8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1. Both the Driver and Constructor championships are decided by total points across all races in the season.

sections.question: How many points does the race winner receive?
sections.explanation: The race winner receives 25 points — the highest awarded for a single finishing position.

modules.title: Qualifying Format
modules.subtitle: Three knockout sessions for the starting grid

sections.content: Qualifying has three knockout rounds: Q1 (20 minutes, all 20 drivers, bottom 5 eliminated), Q2 (15 minutes, 15 drivers, bottom 5 eliminated), Q3 (12 minutes, top 10 shootout for pole position).
Pole position (P1 on the grid) is a significant advantage. The driver starts at the front, has clean air, and avoids first-lap chaos. Historically, ~40% of races are won from pole.

sections.text: Ayrton Senna holds the single-season record for pole positions: 13 poles in a 16-race season (1989). Lewis Hamilton holds the all-time record with 104+ poles.

sections.question: How many drivers are eliminated in Q1?
sections.explanation: 5 drivers are eliminated in Q1 (positions 16-20), leaving 15 for Q2.
